ShareShare article

# New inflation data drops tomorrow. Be ready with Sensible Emily's hip pocket guide to the CPI

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/307a5d1c7aff8a518b4770599bd5334f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3171&cropW=3171&xPos=0&yPos=213&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
E\\
\\
By Emily Stewart](https://www.abc.net.au/news/emily-stewart/4506536)
- [Topic:Personal Finance](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/personal-finance)

5h ago5 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 2:58pm

![GRAPHIC Emily Stewart falling notes](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ffc62844032c37154945aaa1b1f7b03d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=911&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=37&width=862&height=485)

_Hi, I'm_ [_Emily Stewart_](https://www.abc.net.au/news/emily-stewart/4506536) _and I'm here to help you over the next couple of weeks as we navigate some of the **important moments** in economic news that could **affect you**._ 🫶

_The war in Iran has caused so much **uncertainty** globally and I know it can be an **anxious** time as we hear about how the oil crisis has affected us here in Australia._ 😟

_Some of those effects are immediately obvious, like the higher price of petrol. But some things take time to become clear — like the impact on **inflation** figures, **interest rates** and the **federal budget.**_ 💰

_I'm here to help you understand what's happening. If you have a question for me,_ [_**please reach out**_](https://yourquestions.abc.net.au/hc/en-au/requests/new?ticket_form_id=13196881880719) _. I'd love to answer your questions_. 🤔

## What is the key moment I should look out for on inflation?

- **What:** The Australian Bureau of Statistics will release the latest **Consumer Price Index** (CPI) data
- **When:** Tomorrow (April 29)
- **Why is this important?** The CPI tracks how much we pay for a hypothetical "basket" of goods and services we have to buy regularly. 🧺 It matters because it's what the Reserve Bank looks at when it's deciding whether to increase or decrease interest rates.

![GRAPHIC Basket of goods on green](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c47180aac18c33b64e16e649833d6bac?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

## What is the Consumer Price Index?

- **What is it?** The CPI is the official measure of household inflation and tracks changes in the **cost of living** for consumers. 💵 It looks at a **broad range of products** and services, including housing, food and education. The Australian Bureau of Statistics collects the data monthly using supermarket scanning data, web scraping and even phone collection from retailers. As of October last year, it **publishes the data monthly**, to give everyone a "quicker read" on inflation. However, monthly data is more volatile than quarterly figures (so take it with a grain of salt🧂).
- **Why does it matter?** It not only confirms our fears about how much products and services have increased in price, but it can also ultimately have a big **impact** on how much we end up paying in **interest on our mortgage** 🏠 (or how much interest we earn on savings).
- **How could it affect me?** If prices climb faster than your income, you can't afford to buy as much. This isn't good news for business either. So, the Reserve Bank tries to keep inflation in the 2-3 per cent range. If it starts getting too high, the bank might decide to increase interest rates to try and encourage Australians to spend less.

All groups Consumer Price Index (CPI) and trimmed mean, Australia, annual (%)

### All groups Consumer Price Index (CPI) and trimmed mean, Australia, annual (%)

Shaded area: the Reserve Bank's 2-3% target range for inflation

Two lines showing how inflation has risen and fallen over the last four years.

All

groups CPI (%)

Trimmed

mean (%)

012345673.73.3

"All groups CPI" refers to headline inflation. The "trimmed mean" is the RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation. Annual movements prior to April 2025 are calculated by comparing each quarter to the same quarter in the previous year. From April 2025 these movements are calculated by comparing each month to the same month in the previous year.

Source:Chart: ABC News Source: Australian Bureau of StatisticsGet the data

## Should I be worried?

Economists (and financial markets) will be taking **particular interest** in tomorrow's CPI data because it's the first set of data that will **capture the impact of the oil price spike** caused by the war in Iran.⛽

The **CPI rose 3.7 per cent in February** (down slightly from 3.8 per cent in January). Underlying inflation, (RBA's preferred measure because it strips out large price movements) was steady at 3.3 per cent. However, that is **already higher than the Reserve Bank's target range**(see the graph above). Now, the increased **fuel prices** are likely to **push March inflation higher** again. (We've actually just seen this happen in the UK💂).

So, most economists reckon the Reserve Bank is **likely to raise interest rates** again in May, in order to get inflation back down. However, don't forget **inflation is only one key set of data**. The RBA will also be looking at **unemployment** figures and **economic growth** to decide on the official cash rate.💸

Some economists are already warning that we could be starting to see something called " [**stagflation**](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-22/what-is-stagflation-and-what-can-we-do-about-it/106472996)" (a stagnant economy with high inflation). However, others believe the oil price spike might only cause a short-lived inflation peak.📈

So, I think at this stage, **be alert, but not alarmed**. If inflation increases, interest rates are likely to as well. Try and adjust your budget and shop around for the best deal.🛒

## What is the next key moment to watch for?

- **What:** The Reserve Bank will meet next week and announce whether or not **interest rates** will rise.
- **When:** Tuesday May 5 at 2.30pm AEST
- **Why is it important?** The official cash rate decision flows through to how much your bank charges you in interest on your mortgage, or pays you in interest on your savings.

_I'll be back soon to help you understand the CPI announcement. Until then, you can_ [_**follow me for more**_](https://www.abc.net.au/newsletters?isfeatured=abc-your-money-explained) _personal finance news_.

Disclaimer: This information is just general in nature. If you need personalised financial advice, please see a professional.

# Program: Data and decisions are coming

[Program:ABC Business Daily](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/programs/abc-business-daily/)

19h ago19 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 12:15am

Space to play or pause, M to mute, left and right arrows to seek, up and down arrows for volume.
Play

Duration: 24 minutes 24m

Presented by

[![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1281a840902e69e16ebaa505669761bf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=531&cropW=531&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=862)\\
\\
Carrington Clarke](https://www.abc.net.au/news/carrington-clarke/8042208)[![ABC business reporter Daniel Ziffer in a front on profile shot wearing a grey suit and stripy tie.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3d89f0464a3f26aa3c31950b5f4e036a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=400&cropW=400&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=862)\\
\\
Daniel Ziffer](https://www.abc.net.au/news/daniel-ziffer/8957386)

For watchers of the Australian economy, it’s off to the races, with important data and decisions on the horizon.

Wednesday's inflation numbers will provide crucial information for the RBA’s interest rate decision expected next month.

And in the United States, five of the so-called Magnificent Seven tech companies, are set to release their results this week, causing investors to revisit questions about the valuation of AI.

**Carrington Clarke** and **ABC Business Reporter Daniel Ziffer** break it all down on _ABC Business Daily_.

###### **Got a burning business question?**

We'd love to hear your questions! If there’s business and economic news that has you stumped or you'd like further insight into, we're here to help. Send a short voice recording to Carrington and the team at [abcbusinessdaily@abc.net.au](mailto:thepartyroom@abc.net.au) and we'll attempt to answer it.

- [Carrington Clarke, Presenter](https://www.abc.net.au/news/carrington-clarke/8042208)

- [Jessica Lukjanow, Supervising Producer](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/jessica-lukjanow/10427488)

[Skip to main content](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/business/series/0/video/NC2606H049S00#content) [Skip to iview navigation, settings and search](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/business/series/0/video/NC2606H049S00#mastheadLogoAnchor)

![](https://cdn.iview.abc.net.au/thumbs/1200/nc/NC2606H_696f1418022f9.jpg)

### Monday 27/4/2026Monday 27/4/2026

[![](https://iview.abc.net.au/img/placeholder.svg)\\
\\
Duration: 15 minutes 15m](https://iview.abc.net.au/video/NC2606H049S00)

Health insurance giant Bupa accused of anti-competitive behaviour in its dealings with private hospitals. Plus the Middle East war oil shock - fears fossil fuels are taking the focus off the push for renewables.

including

This episode was published 15 hours ago, available until 7:00pm on 27 May 2026.

### Thursday 23/4/2026Thursday 23/4/2026

[![](https://iview.abc.net.au/img/placeholder.svg)\\

Duration: 15 minutes 15m](https://iview.abc.net.au/video/NC2606H048S00)

metadata.og:description: Health insurance giant Bupa accused of anti-competitive behaviour in its dealings with private hospitals. Plus the Middle East war oil shock - fears fossil fuels are taking the focus off the push for renewables.
